context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigations — Decision-making Actors

Recommended due date: June 27 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners, for peer learning.

Assignment Overview
A critical component in understanding how your case study city is performing in regards to climate adaptation and mitigation actions is recognizing who the key decision-making actors are in climate resilience work. Identify and analyze key decision-makers involved in climate action in your case study city.

Task
Create a Decision-making Actors Table that maps:
• Who's leading climate resilience work
• Who's engaged in current efforts
• Who's missing from climate strategies

Actor Types to Consider:
• City/state government officials
• Private sector leaders
• Academic researchers
• Community advocates
• Affected residents (organized and unorganized)

Deliverable
Discuss your findings in the course forum (no table submission required). Focus on which sectors are most active and identify gaps in participation.

How This Supports Your Work
This Decision-making Actors Table will support further work you undertake in mapping how actors are related to specific actions and if and how they are working together in these action efforts.

Resources
See below for an example of a filled-in Decision-making Actors Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk i'm kinda lost on this city case study thing rn, can u just explain the whole decision-making actors part from the start?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you work through it! Before I jump in, let me ask: have you already picked a case study city? That's the starting point for everything here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation, which would violate the guided discovery principle. Instead, I should orient them gently by asking what they already know or have done, so I can meet them where they are and use the Socratic method to move forward. I'll keep it bite-sized and not overwhelm them.

exchanges.student: uh maybe, but tbh i'm confused about the assignment more than the city rn. can u just give me the big picture first?
exchanges.tutor: Fair enough — let's tackle the confusion. What part of the assignment description feels unclear to you? Try reading it once more and tell me what you think the main task is asking you to do.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still asking me to explain things for them rather than engaging actively. I should redirect by asking a targeted question that gets them to read and engage with the assignment themselves, while acknowledging their confusion. I'll keep it short and Socratic.

exchanges.student: ngl it all kinda blurs together. i think it's like a table about who does climate stuff, but i'm not really sure what counts or how detailed it should be.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a decent starting point! Now, the assignment gives you three specific things the table should map. Can you go back and find those three things? That'll sharpen your sense of what belongs in it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually grasped the basic idea reasonably well ('a table about who does climate stuff'). I should affirm that partial understanding and use a Socratic question to push them to be more specific about what the table is meant to capture, using the three bullet points from the assignment as a guide without spelling them out for them.

exchanges.student: i saw that part but now i'm second guessing it lol. can u just walk me through the whole table structure real quick?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but that's your job, not mine! You said you saw the three things the table maps — just tell me what they are. You're closer than you think.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the work for them. I should hold my ground, remind them of their role, and redirect back to the specific question I already asked. They've admitted they saw the three things — I should push them to actually state what they found rather than giving in.

exchanges.student: maybe it's who's leading, who's involved, and who's missing? but idk how i'm supposed to analyze that in the forum post fr.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — good. So now the real question: which city are you working with? You can't fill in any of those three things without one.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the three mapping elements. I should affirm this clearly and then move them forward toward the next logical step: picking a city, since without a city they can't actually do the analysis. I'll keep it short and push them toward action.

exchanges.student: i haven't fully locked it in yet, which is why this is messing me up tbh. if i pick one, can u just explain what i'd be looking for in each column?
exchanges.tutor: Picking your city is the first move — everything else follows from that. What cities are you considering? Even a rough idea is fine.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to pre-explain the work before doing it. I should redirect — picking the city is the prerequisite and I shouldn't let them avoid that step. I'll push them to commit to a city first, then we can work from there using Socratic questions about that specific city.

exchanges.student: maybe jakarta or boston, idk. i'm still fuzzy tho, like what even makes someone a "decision-making actor" here?
exchanges.tutor: Good, pick one — Jakarta or Boston, doesn't matter much right now. Then think about this: if your city needed to make a decision about flooding, who would actually have the power or influence to shape that decision? Start there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress by narrowing to two cities, but is now asking a conceptual question to avoid committing. Rather than defining 'decision-making actor' for them, I should ask a Socratic question that gets them to reason it out themselves. I'll also gently nudge them to pick a city.

exchanges.student: ok but that's where i get lost again bc it feels like literally everyone lol. can u pls just explain from scratch how to tell leaders vs engaged vs missing?
exchanges.tutor: You're circling the same question — let's break out of it. Pick Boston or Jakarta right now. Then give me ONE example of someone you think is leading climate work there and tell me WHY you think they qualify. Trust your instincts and we'll refine from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles and asking me to explain the same conceptual territory again. This is the third time they're asking me to explain things rather than try themselves. I should be more direct and push them to attempt an answer using their own reasoning, perhaps with a concrete prompt about their chosen city. I should also nudge them to commit to a city.

exchanges.student: uhh boston i guess. maybe the city gov? idk why exactly, can u just tell me what kind of reasons count?
exchanges.tutor: Boston, good. Now think about it: what does a city government actually *do* that would put them in the 'leading' category rather than just 'engaged'? What's the difference in terms of power or responsibility?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has picked Boston and identified city government as a potential leader — that's real progress. But they're still asking me to feed them the reasoning. I should affirm the choice and push them to generate their own reasoning with a targeted Socratic question rather than explaining what 'counts.'

exchanges.student: i'm not sure tbh, that's what i'm asking 😭 can u just give me a simple example setup for boston so i can see the difference?
exchanges.tutor: I get the frustration, but I won't give you an example setup — that's your work to do. Try this: who *sets* climate policy in Boston versus who *responds* to it? That difference might be your answer.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is frustrated and asking for an example setup, which would essentially be giving them partial solution. I need to hold my ground but also recognize they may be genuinely stuck on the leading vs. engaged distinction. Rather than giving an example, I can ask a simple concrete question that gets them to reason it out themselves. I should be encouraging but firm.

deductions.reason: In several turns (e.g., Turns 8–10), when the student repeatedly expresses confusion about the same concept (leaders vs. engaged vs. missing), the tutor continues asking similar Socratic questions without first diagnosing *why* the student is stuck. The tutor does not probe what the student already understands about power, governance, or policy before re-asking. A brief diagnostic question about the student's prior knowledge would have been more appropriate before cycling through similar prompts.

The tutor provides minimal method-level feedback on the student's thinking process. When the student correctly identifies the three mapping categories (Turn 5), the tutor simply confirms and moves on without explaining *why* that structure matters or how to use it methodologically. Feedback throughout is largely directional ('pick a city') rather than process-oriented (e.g., how to evaluate actor influence, how to distinguish categories analytically).

The tutor cycles on the 'pick a city' prompt across Turns 5–8 without consolidating or advancing. The student repeatedly signals confusion about the conceptual distinction between actor categories, but the tutor keeps redirecting to city selection rather than reframing or simplifying the conceptual issue. By Turn 8, the student is still stuck on the same question and the tutor has not meaningfully advanced the dialogue.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently avoids doing the student's work and maintains a Socratic stance throughout, never providing submission-ready content. The main weaknesses are: (1) a repetitive loop around city selection that fails to address the student's actual conceptual confusion about actor categories, triggering a redundancy deduction; (2) insufficient diagnostic questioning before re-prompting when the student is stuck; and (3) limited method-level feedback on how to think analytically about actor roles. Communication is appropriately concise and tone is supportive throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks — the tutor never gives direct answers, rewrites student work, or reveals conclusions before student reasoning. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because the tutor repeatedly re-asks similar questions (Turns 8–10) without first diagnosing what specifically the student misunderstands about actor categories. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.b because the tutor's feedback is directional rather than process-oriented; when the student correctly identifies the three categories, no method-level guidance is offered on how to apply them analytically. 2.1 loses 4 points under 2.1.A.a because the tutor cycles on 'pick a city' across multiple turns without reframing or synthesizing, while the student's real confusion about conceptual distinctions goes unaddressed. 2.2 and section 3 have no deductions — the tutor keeps the student anchored to the assignment and communicates clearly and supportively.
